disease.name: Cholelithiasis / Gallbladder Stones
disease.note: Calculi within the gallbladder. Often asymptomatic but can cause biliary colic, cholecystitis, pancreatitis, or cholangitis. The '4F' stereotype (Fat, Female, Forty, Fertile) remains clinically useful. In East Africa, prevalence is rising with increasing obesity, high-fat diets, and metabolic syndrome.
presentingComplaints: Intermittent right upper abdominal pain after eating
Pain lasting 30 minutes to a few hours then going away
Pain that spreads to the right shoulder or between shoulder blades
Nausea and bloating after fatty meals
Accidentally found on ultrasound with no symptoms
pathophysiologyDetail: Cholesterol supersaturation: bile contains cholesterol, bile salts, and phospholipids (lecithin); when cholesterol exceeds the solubilizing capacity of bile salts + lecithin → supersaturation → cholesterol crystal nucleation → stone growth

Biliary sludge: cholesterol crystals, calcium bilirubinate granules, and mucin gel aggregate in the gallbladder → sludge forms in hypomotile or stagnant gallbladder → can progress to stones or resolve spontaneously
Gallbladder hypomotility: pregnancy, rapid weight loss, TPN, prolonged fasting → reduced gallbladder emptying → bile stasis → prolonged contact time → enhanced crystal nucleation and stone growth
Mucin hypersecretion: inflamed or stagnant gallbladder epithelium secretes excess mucin → mucin gel acts as a scaffold for cholesterol crystal nucleation and aggregation → accelerates stone formation

Brown pigment stones: formed in infected bile with bacterial β-glucuronidase → deconjugates bilirubin glucuronide → insoluble unconjugated bilirubin precipitates with calcium → brown, muddy stones; associated with cholangitis and biliary stasis

Black pigment stones: sterile bile with increased unconjugated bilirubin from hemolysis (sickle cell, thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis) or ineffective erythropoiesis → calcium bilirubinate polymers → small, hard, black, multiple faceted stones

Biliary colic pathophysiology: stone transiently impacts at the gallbladder neck or cystic duct → gallbladder contracts against obstruction → increased wall tension → visceral pain mediated by splanchnic nerves → pain resolves when stone falls back or passes

Gallstone pancreatitis: small cholesterol or pigment stones (<5 mm) pass through the cystic duct and ampulla of Vater → transient obstruction of the pancreatic duct → pancreatic duct hypertension → premature activation of pancreatic enzymes → acute pancreatitis

Natural history of asymptomatic stones: 10-25% become symptomatic at 5 years, 2-3% per year develop biliary colic; risk of complications (cholecystitis, pancreatitis, cholangitis) is 0.5-1% per year; 50% of asymptomatic patients remain symptom-free lifelong

Gallstone ileus pathophysiology: large stone (>2.5 cm) erodes through gallbladder wall into adjacent bowel (usually duodenum → cholecystoduodenal fistula) → stone passes distally → impacts at narrowest point (terminal ileum, ileocecal valve) → mechanical small bowel obstruction

Porcelain gallbladder: chronic inflammation → dystrophic calcification of the gallbladder wall → blue-discolored, brittle gallbladder on gross examination; historically associated with gallbladder cancer (10-25%) but contemporary studies suggest lower risk (2-5%)

Gallbladder polyps: cholesterol polyps (most common, benign, <10 mm), inflammatory polyps, adenomyomatosis, adenomatous polyps (premalignant); risk of carcinoma increases with size (>10 mm: 10-50% malignant risk), sessile morphology, solitary, and patient age >50

Mirizzi syndrome: stone impacted in the gallbladder neck or cystic duct → extrinsic compression of the common hepatic duct → obstructive jaundice; Type I: compression without fistula; Types II-IV: cholecystocholedochal fistula with varying degrees of bile duct erosion

East African context: rising prevalence with urbanization and Western dietary patterns; obesity in Kenyan women 27%; hemolytic anemias (sickle cell trait 15-20% in coastal regions) contribute to pigment stones; limited access to laparoscopic cholecystectomy at district hospitals; open cholecystectomy remains common

landmarkTrials.trial: Natural History of Asymptomatic Gallstones (1995, Annals of Internal Medicine — GREPCO Study)
landmarkTrials.population: 29,000+ Italian women screened; 1,511 with asymptomatic gallstones followed for 10 years
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation vs prophylactic cholecystectomy for asymptomatic gallstones
landmarkTrials.outcome: Cumulative probability of developing biliary colic: 12% at 5 years, 18% at 10 years, 26% at 20 years. Complication rate: 2-3% over 10 years. Mortality from gallstone disease: 0.5% over 20 years. Most asymptomatic patients remain asymptomatic.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Supports expectant management for asymptomatic stones in Kenya; surgery only for symptomatic disease

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Cholecystectomy (1992, The Lancet — National Survey)
landmarkTrials.population: Nationwide survey of 77,604 cholecystectomies in the US comparing laparoscopic vs open
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy vs open cholecystectomy (early experience)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Lap: shorter hospital stay (1.6 vs 5.8 days), faster return to work (12 vs 33 days). Bile duct injury: 0.5% lap vs 0.2% open (learning curve). Mortality: 0.04% lap vs 0.1% open.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Established lap chole as gold standard; bile duct injury risk higher during learning curve — relevant for Kenyan surgeons adopting laparoscopy

landmarkTrials.trial: Ursodeoxycholic Acid for Gallstone Dissolution (1989, Gastroenterology — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.intervention: UDCA 8-15 mg/kg/day vs placebo for 2 years
landmarkTrials.outcome: Complete dissolution: 38% UDCA vs 3% placebo. Best results: small (<5mm), floating (cholesterol) stones in functioning gallbladder. Stone recurrence after stopping: 50% at 5 years.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: UDCA available in Kenya; option for selected patients unfit for surgery or declining surgery

landmarkTrials.trial: Single-incision vs Multi-port Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy (2011, Annals of Surgery — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 400 patients randomized to single-incision vs conventional 4-port laparoscopic cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Single-incision laparoscopic cholecystectomy (SILC) vs conventional 4-port LC
landmarkTrials.outcome: SILC: less pain (VAS 3 vs 4, p=0.01), better cosmesis, longer operative time (65 vs 52 min). Similar complication rates. Higher incisional hernia rate with SILC (3% vs 0.5% at 1 year).
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Conventional 4-port LC standard in Kenya; SILC not widely available

landmarkTrials.trial: Cholecystectomy vs Observation for Silent Gallstones in Patients with Diabetes (1999, Annals of Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: 711 diabetic patients with asymptomatic gallstones followed for 5 years
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in mortality or gallstone-related complications. Observation is safe even in diabetics. Prophylactic cholecystectomy not indicated for asymptomatic gallstones in diabetes.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Even in diabetics — observation is safe for asymptomatic stones; no need for prophylactic surgery

landmarkTrials.trial: Gallstone Prevention in Rapid Weight Loss (1995, New England Journal of Medicine — Ursodeoxycholic Acid)
landmarkTrials.population: 100 patients undergoing rapid weight loss (very low calorie diet)
landmarkTrials.intervention: UDCA 600 mg/day vs placebo during weight loss period
landmarkTrials.outcome: Gallstone formation: 3% UDCA vs 28% placebo (p<0.001). UDCA prevents gallstone formation during rapid weight loss. Benefit persists only while taking UDCA.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Relevant for Kenyan bariatric surgery programs and patients on very low calorie diets

landmarkTrials.trial: Cholecystectomy for Gallstone Pancreatitis (2002, Gastroenterology — PONCHO Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 108 patients with mild gallstone pancreatitis randomized to cholecystectomy during index admission vs interval
landmarkTrials.intervention: Same-admission cholecystectomy vs interval cholecystectomy after 6 weeks
landmarkTrials.outcome: Same-admission: 0% recurrent biliary events vs 30% in interval group (biliary colic, cholecystitis, recurrent pancreatitis). Same-admission cholecystectomy safe and prevents recurrence.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Same-admission cholecystectomy after mild pancreatitis strongly recommended in Kenya

evidenceTable.effect: 10-25% become symptomatic over 5-10 years; complication risk 0.5-1%/year

evidenceTable.recommendation: UDCA for selected patients unfit for surgery

evidenceTable.therapy: Prophylactic cholecystectomy in diabetics with asymptomatic stones
evidenceTable.effect: No difference in outcomes vs observation
evidenceTable.recommendation: Observation safe even in diabetics with asymptomatic stones

evidenceTable.therapy: UDCA for gallstone prevention during rapid weight loss
evidenceTable.population: Patients on very low calorie diet
evidenceTable.recommendation: UDCA 600mg/day during rapid weight loss to prevent stones

evidenceTable.population: Suspected CBD stones (elevated LFTs, dilated CBD)
evidenceTable.recommendation: MRCP non-invasive test of choice for CBD stones

evidenceTable.effect: Sensitivity 95%, specificity 97%; superior to MRCP for small stones
evidenceTable.recommendation: EUS for high-sensitivity detection; reserved for equivocal cases

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical assessment — classic biliary colic: intermittent postprandial RUQ pain lasting 30 min to 6 hours, radiating to right shoulder/back, associated with nausea, non-specific dyspepsia

Step 2: Abdominal ultrasound — gold standard for diagnosis. Identify stones (hyperechoic with acoustic shadowing), gallbladder wall thickness, sludge, polyp, CBD diameter, and sonographic Murphy sign. Sensitivity >95% for stones >2 mm
Step 3: Laboratory — LFTs (ALP, GGT, bilirubin) to assess for CBD obstruction; amylase/lipase to rule out pancreatitis; CBC for leukocytosis suggesting cholecystitis

Step 4: Risk stratification for CBD stones — predictors: dilated CBD >6 mm, bilirubin >1.8 mg/dL, ALP >2× ULN, gallstone pancreatitis, cholangitis, age >55. High probability (any predictor): MRCP or EUS before cholecystectomy
Step 5: MRCP — non-invasive imaging of biliary tree. Sensitivity 93% for CBD stones. Preferred for intermediate-risk patients. Avoids unnecessary ERCP and its complications
Step 6: EUS — high sensitivity (95%) for small CBD stones (<5 mm). Alternative to MRCP; can be done in the same session as ERCP if stone found
Step 7: ERCP — therapeutic only for confirmed CBD stones. Performed before or during cholecystectomy. Single-stage ERCP + cholecystectomy reduces hospital stay
Step 8: CT abdomen — low sensitivity for stones but useful for complications (perforation, abscess, mass) and alternative diagnoses
Step 9: HIDA scan — rarely needed; useful when ultrasound is equivocal for acalculous biliary disease; non-visualization of gallbladder = cystic duct obstruction

Step 10: Treatment decision — asymptomatic: observe. Symptomatic biliary colic: elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Complicated disease (cholecystitis, pancreatitis, cholangitis): urgent management per specific protocol
Kenya adaptation: US widely available; MRCP at major referral centers; LFTs + US clinical prediction sufficient for CBD stone risk stratification at district level; ERCP only at tertiary centers
managementPearls: Asymptomatic gallstones found incidentally on imaging need NO treatment — the risk of developing complications is only 0.5-1% per year; the risk of prophylactic cholecystectomy (bile duct injury 0.3-0.5% even in expert hands) exceeds the natural history risk

Biliary colic vs cholecystitis: colic lasts <6 hours and the patient is pain-free between episodes; cholecystitis lasts >6 hours with persistent tenderness, fever, and leukocytosis — this distinction determines urgency

Preoperative LFTs and CBD measurement on US are mandatory before cholecystectomy — a normal CBD and normal LFTs virtually exclude CBD stones (NPV >95%); abnormal LFTs with dilated CBD require MRCP/EUS

Intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) should be performed selectively: dilated CBD, small stones in gallbladder, history of jaundice/pancreatitis, elevated LFTs, or unclear anatomy — 'a picture of safety is worth a thousand clips'
Gallstone pancreatitis requires cholecystectomy during the same admission once pancreatitis resolves (typically 2-5 days) — waiting >6 weeks results in 30% recurrent biliary events (PONCHO trial)
Patients with retained CBD stones after cholecystectomy should be managed with ERCP and sphincterotomy; laparoscopic CBD exploration is an alternative for centers with expertise
Post-cholecystectomy diarrhea occurs in 5-15% of patients due to bile acid malabsorption — usually self-limiting but can be treated with cholestyramine or bile acid binders if persistent

In sickle cell disease: routine screening for gallstones with US starting at age 10; cholecystectomy for symptomatic stones; prophylactic cholecystectomy is controversial but may be considered in selected high-risk patients

Gallbladder polyps >10 mm require cholecystectomy regardless of symptoms — the risk of malignancy in polyps 10-15 mm is 10-30%, increasing to >50% for >15 mm; polyps 6-9 mm need surveillance US at 6, 12, 24 months

In pregnancy: symptomatic gallstones are managed conservatively (low-fat diet, avoid triggers); urgent cholecystectomy reserved for complicated disease (cholecystitis, pancreatitis) — laparoscopic cholecystectomy is safe in the second trimester

Ursodeoxycholic acid (13-15 mg/kg/day) can dissolve small (<5 mm) radiolucent cholesterol stones in a functioning gallbladder over 6-24 months — but recurrence after discontinuation is 50% at 5 years, making it suitable only for patients who refuse or cannot have surgery
operativeNuances: Critical view of safety (CVS) before clipping any structure: Calot's triangle is completely cleared of fat, the gallbladder infundibulum is retracted laterally away from the CBD, only two structures enter the gallbladder, and the cystic plate (liver bed) is visible behind the gallbladder neck

In obese patients (BMI >35), consider a supraumbilical or left upper quadrant camera port to avoid the pannus; use long (40 cm) instruments; higher insufflation pressure (15-17 mmHg) may be needed for adequate exposure

Bail-out techniques for the difficult gallbladder: (1) subtotal cholecystectomy (leave posterior wall adherent to liver), (2) fundus-first approach (dome-down), (3) open conversion — knowing when to bail out is the mark of a wise surgeon

Retract the infundibulum LATERALLY (not medially) to open the angle between the cystic duct and CBD; medial retraction aligns the cystic duct with the CBD, giving the false impression that all structures are 'normal' and leading to the classic 'tenting' bile duct injury

The infundibulum-taper sign: the cystic duct should smoothly taper from the gallbladder infundibulum to its junction; if another duct (CBD) appears to also arise from the infundibulum, stop — this is an abnormal junction and bile duct injury is imminent

In acute cholecystitis with a frozen Calot's triangle, place a 22-gauge spinal needle into the distended gallbladder through the fundus and aspirate bile/decompressed gallbladder first — this enables grasping and retraction, reducing the risk of gallbladder perforation during dissection

Subtotal cholecystectomy technique: transect the gallbladder at the body-infundibulum junction; leave the posterior wall of the infundibulum intact on the liver bed; close the infundibular opening with a running 3-0 PDS suture — do not attempt to identify the cystic duct in frozen tissue

Rouviere's sulcus is the most reliable laparoscopic landmark for safe cholecystectomy — it is a groove between the right lobe and caudate lobe; the common bile duct always runs BEHIND a right-angle plane drawn through this sulcus; dissection should always stay ANTERIOR to this plane

In East African settings where electrocautery units, clip appliers, and retrieval bags may be limited, open cholecystectomy through a right subcostal (Kocher) incision remains a safe and efficient alternative — world-class outcomes can be achieved without any laparoscopic equipment

Cystic duct closure: for a broad cystic duct (>5 mm), use an endoloop or suture ligation instead of clips (which may not fully across a thick duct); a 'redundant' cystic duct stump can later form a 'recurrent' stone (cystic duct stump syndrome)

prognosis.asymptomaticStones: 10-25% develop symptoms over 5-10 years; complication rate 0.5-1% per year; most remain asymptomatic lifelong
prognosis.symptomaticBiliaryColic: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy resolves symptoms in 90-95%; 5-10% have persistent symptoms (post-cholecystectomy syndrome — investigate other causes)
prognosis.gallstonePancreatitis: Mild attack: mortality <1%; severe attack: mortality 15-25%; cholecystectomy during same admission prevents recurrent pancreatitis (30% without cholecystectomy)
prognosis.retainedCbdStone: ERCP with stone extraction: 90-95% success; 5-10% need second procedure or surgical CBD exploration
prognosticFactors: Stone size — large stones (>2 cm) increase cancer risk; small stones (<5 mm) increase pancreatitis risk
Stone composition — cholesterol stones may dissolve with UDCA; pigment stones do not respond to UDCA
Gallbladder function — non-functioning gallbladder (non-visualization on HIDA) predicts poor response to UDCA
Stones in CBD — increase morbidity and need for additional procedures (ERCP)
Number of stones — multiple small stones increase risk of CBD migration and pancreatitis
Concurrent pregnancy — increases risk of cholecystitis, limits surgical options to second trimester
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Gallstone disease increasing in Kenya with dietary transitions; open cholecystectomy still common at district hospitals; laparoscopic cholecystectomy available at county and national referral hospitals; pigment stones from hemolytic anemias (sickle cell) more prevalent in coastal and western Kenya; late presentation with complicated disease is common

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Complicated stone disease (pancreatitis/cholangitis/cholecystitis) -> SEVERE; asymptomatic or biliary colic -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you get pain in your right upper abdomen after eating?
Does the pain come and go in waves?
How long does the pain typically last?
Does the pain spread to your right shoulder or back?
Do certain foods trigger the pain (fatty, fried, oily)?
Do you feel bloated or nauseous after meals?
Have you ever been jaundiced (yellow eyes)?
Have you ever had pancreatitis (severe epigastric pain)?
Do you have known gallstones from a previous ultrasound?
Are you overweight or have you gained weight recently?
Have you been pregnant or given birth?
Have you lost a lot of weight rapidly recently?
Do you have diabetes?
Does anyone in your family have gallstones?
Do you take any medications including traditional herbs?
Do you eat a diet of ugali, sukuma wiki, and fried foods?
Have you had any blood transfusions in the past?
Are you taking contraceptive pills or hormone therapy?
Do you have sickle cell disease or trait? (pigment stone risk)
Do you have Crohn's disease or have you had ileal surgery? (bile salt malabsorption)
Have you been on long-term total parenteral nutrition (TPN)?
Do you have thalassemia or hereditary spherocytosis?
Do you have cirrhosis of the liver? (pigment stone risk)
Have you had a cholecystectomy before and still have symptoms? (cystic duct stump syndrome)
Is the pain associated with nausea, vomiting, or bloating?
Do you feel the pain as a dull ache or sharp/cramping?
During a pain episode, do you need to stop moving or lie still?
Have you noticed that you cannot tolerate certain foods that you used to eat?
Do you have heartburn or a sour taste in your mouth along with the pain?
Have you been diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)?
Do you take any medications for lowering cholesterol (statins)?
Does the pain ever wake you up from sleep?
Have you recently given birth or are you currently pregnant?
Have you had a cholecystectomy in the past? (biliary pain without GB possible)
Do you feel pain in your chest or between your shoulder blades specifically?
Do you take any traditional herbal remedies for your symptoms?
Is there any history of gallbladder or bile duct cancer in your family?

examination_findings.finding: Right upper quadrant tenderness — mild to moderate (between attacks)
Murphy's sign — positive if acute cholecystitis has developed
Palpable gallbladder — Courvoisier sign if CBD obstructed
Jaundice — present only if stone has passed into CBD
Epigastric tenderness — may be present
Abdominal distension — mild from bloating
Boas sign — hyperesthesia at right infrascapular area
Fever — only if acute cholecystitis has developed
Tachycardia — during acute colic episode
Obesity — commonly associated
Xanthelasma or skin tags — associated with metabolic syndrome
RUQ tenderness on deep palpation — mild to moderate between attacks
Murphy sign: if positive, suggests acute cholecystitis (not simple biliary colic)
Obesity (BMI >30) — commonly associated metabolic risk factor
Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) — painless, palpable GB suggests malignant CBD obstruction rather than simple stones
Jaundice — scleral icterus visible when bilirubin >2.5 mg/dL; suggests CBD stone or Mirizzi
Fever — suggests progression to cholecystitis or cholangitis; not present in simple colic
Tachycardia — during an acute biliary colic episode from pain
Boas sign: right infrascapular hyperesthesia — referred visceral pain from gallbladder
Abdominal distension from bloating during episodes
Right shoulder tip tenderness on deep RUQ palpation (referred phrenic)
Skin: xanthelasma, acanthosis nigricans (metabolic syndrome markers)
Pale conjunctiva — if chronic anemia from underlying hemolytic condition
Scratch marks on skin (excoriations) — if pruritus from chronic cholestasis (CBD stone)

bedside_scores.name: Gallstone Risk Score (GRS)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score ≥4: high probability of gallstone disease; consider ultrasound

riskFactors.note: Estrogen increases cholesterol saturation in bile
Increased cholesterol secretion, decreased bile salts
Estrogen + progesterone cause bile stasis and increased cholesterol
Increased pigment stone formation from hemolysis
Sickle cell, thalassemia — increased bilirubin load → pigment stones

subtypes.name: Cholesterol Stones
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic stones. Expectant management for asymptomatic. Ursodeoxycholic acid (13-15 mg/kg/day) for selected small (<5mm) radiolucent stones in non-obese patients with functioning gallbladder.

subtypes.name: Pigment Stones (Brown or Black)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Treat underlying cause (hemolysis, infection). Brown stones often associated with biliary infection and may require antibiotics + ERCP for CBD clearance.

mimics.whyMimic: Bloating, abdominal pain, altered bowel habit
RUQ pain, but more severe and persistent
RUQ pain, jaundice, weight loss, Courvoisier sign
Recurrent RUQ/epigastric pain after cholecystectomy for stones
RUQ pain, palpable mass, abnormal LFTs
Right-sided abdominal pain radiating to groin, nausea

bedside.name: Murphy's sign test
bedside.indication: Assess for acute cholecystitis if symptomatic

laboratory.name: Amylase/Lipase
laboratory.indication: Rule out pancreatitis if epigastric pain

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Gold standard — detects stones >2mm, GB wall, CBD size

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: If CBD stones suspected (elevated LFTs, dilated CBD)

imaging.name: CT Abdomen
imaging.indication: Less sensitive than US for gallstones; useful for complications

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: Therapeutic only — for known CBD stones

imaging.name: EUS
imaging.indication: Highly sensitive for CBD stones — alternative to MRCP

resuscitation: NBM if acute pain or vomiting
IV fluids if dehydrated from vomiting or NPO
Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1g 6-hourly, ± NSAIDs (Diclofenac 75mg IM) for acute biliary colic
IV Morphine 2.5-5mg PRN for severe pain
conservative: Avoid: fried foods, fatty meats, creamy sauces, oily foods
Small frequent meals instead of large meals
Weight loss if obese — gradual (1-2 kg/week) to avoid stone precipitation

surgical.timing: Elective for biliary colic; urgent for acute cholecystitis
surgical.preop_workup: LFTs, US, ± MRCP if CBD stone suspected

nonOperative: Expectant management: for asymptomatic gallstones (incidental finding)
Ursodeoxycholic acid: for small (<5mm) cholesterol stones in functioning GB
Observation: 50% of asymptomatic patients will never develop symptoms

general: Normal diet day 1 — low fat initially

pathways.criteria: Incidental finding on imaging, no biliary colic ever
pathways.treatment: Expectant management. No surgery indicated unless high risk (large stone >3cm, non-functioning GB, calcified GB — porcelain GB, sickle cell disease).

pathways.criteria: Recurrent RUQ pain, confirmed stones on US, no complications
pathways.treatment: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (elective). Same-day surgery possible. No routine CBD imaging unless elevated LFTs or dilated CBD.
ERCP for CBD stone clearance. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy after resolution of acute episode. Urgent management as per specific complication.

steps: Supine, split-leg position, reverse Trendelenburg with left tilt
Identify and grasp gallbladder fundus, retract cephalad over liver edge
Grasp infundibulum, retract laterally to open Calot's triangle
Dissect peritoneum over cystic duct and cystic artery
Achieve critical view of safety: only two structures entering GB, cystic plate visible
Clip cystic artery (2 proximal, 1 distal) and divide
Clip cystic duct (2 proximal, 1 distal) and divide
Dissect gallbladder from liver bed using cautery
Retrieve GB in specimen bag via epigastric port
Remove ports, close fascia at umbilical site
operative_templates.position: Supine, split-leg, reverse Trendelenburg, left tilt

dischargeCriteria: Patient educated on diet and when to return

patient_education.explanation: Gallstones are hardened deposits of bile that form in your gallbladder — a small pouch beneath the liver that stores bile. Many people with gallstones have no symptoms (incidental). If stones cause pain (biliary colic) or complications (inflammation, pancreatitis), the gallbladder should be removed. The body adapts well without a gallbladder.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the hospital if: pain becomes severe and lasts more than 6 hours, you develop fever with chills, you turn yellow (jaundice), you have dark urine or pale stools, or you vomit persistently.
patient_education.activity: For uncomplicated biliary colic: normal activity. After cholecystectomy: walk same day. Avoid heavy lifting for 2 weeks. Return to work: 1 week (desk job), 3-4 weeks (manual labor). No driving for 1 week after surgery.
patient_education.diet: Avoid fatty, fried, and oily foods that trigger pain. Eat small, frequent meals. After cholecystectomy: start with light foods (uji, soup, tea) and gradually resume normal diet. Some people experience looser stools after surgery — this is normal and usually improves. Limit: samosas, fried fish/chicken, fatty meat, cream, butter, ghee.
patient_education.medication: If prescribed UDCA: take as directed — it dissolves small cholesterol stones over 6-24 months. Pain medication: paracetamol or ibuprofen as needed. After surgery: antibiotics if prescribed.
patient_education.followUp: If asymptomatic: no follow-up needed. If biliary colic: arrange elective cholecystectomy. After surgery: wound check at 2 weeks. If you had jaundice pre-op or CBD stones: MRCP/ERCP follow-up.

before_discharge: Dietary advice: low-fat diet, small frequent meals

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — symptom assessment, elective cholecystectomy scheduling
If LFTs were abnormal: repeat LFTs to ensure normalization
Follow-up after cholecystectomy if surgery was performed

when_to_return: Signs of wound infection after surgery
